data.title: Materialise: The Medical Segment Alone Makes This A Buy
data.description:
Materialise NV (MTLS) is a self-ascribed "additive manufacturing" company based in Belgium. Their main technology is better known as 3D-printing. Materialise operates in three segments: Medical, Software, and Manufacturing. Since the COVID-hype around the company died down and Software and Manufacturing showed steady declines over the years, the Medical segment, which is now the company's biggest segment, has been promising with steady growth.

This article is an introduction to the company and our "Buy" initialization.

Company Overview

Materialise has been around for over 35 years now and has been publicly listed since 2014. The company is a leader in the 3D-printing industry and has a global presence, operating in 21 countries across 5 continents.

The main strength of additive manufacturing lies in the customization. Generally, when mass producing a model or component, the cost per model of traditional manufacturing methods is much cheaper. The fixed costs of designing production processes and producing molds become cheaper per model when producing a lot of models. When producing custom models, however, the costs of designing a production line and molds make the price per model shoot through the roof.

Using additive manufacturing, Materialise can help their clients build custom models rapidly and at reasonable costs. In manufacturing, this can mean that extremely specialized, low-volume products can benefit from the low overhead of additive manufacturing. This could be relevant for aerospace applications, for instance, something management has talked about recently. Another use case in their manufacturing segment is prototyping. Additive manufacturing allows for rapid iterations and low-cost production, allowing companies to quickly iterate prototypes.

These advantages over traditional manufacturing are even more pronounced in the medical segment, where Materialise helps doctors design and manufacture custom products for their patients, ensuring a perfect fit. Using traditional processes, manufacturing custom implants is extremely lengthy and expensive. Using additive manufacturing, however, the price per implant and production time can be drastically lower.

Their software segment sells software to design products and run the printers to 3rd parties. This is the same software they use internally, and with EBITDA from this segment essentially flat, it could be more seen as a way for the company to recoup some of the costs they incur from building the software they need anyways. This is the least relevant segment of the three.

Financials

On a consolidated level, revenue in the last 5 years has been only growing slowly, with the last 2 years being essentially flat. The same thing applies to Adjusted EBITDA, hovering around €30m for essentially all years since 2021.

Looking at the segments, however, reveals a more mixed picture. The Medical segment shows consistent growth in the mid- to high-teens with a 4Y CAGR of 16%, with the Software and Manufacturing segments being weak, especially in the last 2 years. On the profit side, the software segment and manufacturing segment are essentially operating at break-even, with some declines in recent years.

In addition to profitable operations, the company has a fortress balance sheet, posting €134m in cash against just €63m in gross debt, resulting in a net cash position of €71m.

Based on the 2026 guidance revealed in their Q4 2025 earnings release, the company expects strong growth in the medical segment to continue, while software and manufacturing will continue to stabilize. On a consolidated level, they expect FY2026E revenues to be between €273m and €283m and AEBIT to be between €10m and €12m.

Valuation

With a share price currently around €4.5, around 58.5m shares outstanding, and net cash of €71m, MTLS has an Enterprise Value of around €192m as of writing. Based on an FY2025 EBITDA of €31m and FY2025 Sales of €268m, MTLS is currently valued at an EV/Sales of around 0.71x and an EV/EBITDA of around 6.2x.

Compared with the biggest public 3D-printing company, Xometry (XMTR), trading at a FWD EV/Sales ratio of over 3x and a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio of over 50x, we still believe that, despite much lower growth, MTLS is undervalued currently.

A look into Seeking Alpha's list of Health Care Equipment stocks also reveals that many growing Health Care Equipment companies trade at valuations closer to EV/Sales of 3x and EV/EBITDA of 10x.

As MTLS' medical segment grows while the other segments stay flat, the company increasingly becomes weighted towards their medical segment. In our opinion, a stronger differentiation, higher growth, and a bigger runway of the medical segment make that segment extremely attractive.

Adjusting for €3m in unallocated HoldCo costs based on FY2025 numbers and assuming similar growth for the medical segment, we estimate FY2026E revenue and AEBITDA for the medical segment to be around €150m and €45m, respectively.

Conveniently, an EV/Sales ratio of 3x and an EV/AEBITDA ratio of 10x for the medical segment alone would result in a valuation of the company of €450m. To be overly conservative, we will discount this valuation by -€100m to reflect the potential for losses in the software and/or manufacturing segments. This assumes the worst case of accelerated losses of those segments to the tune of €10m a year (and a 10x multiplier based on our EV/AEBITDA target ratio).

This leaves us with a target EV of €350m. Adding the €71m in cash and dividing by 58.5m shares results in a target valuation of €7.20 per share, which translates to around $8.40 per share for the ADRs, or over 55% higher than the current price per share.

Risk And What To Look Out For

As the crown jewel of the company, one big risk is the potential end of growth of the medical segment's top-line and/or declining profitability. Even though growth has been largely steady for 5 years, growth somewhat declined in the last 2 years. There is also little visibility into the growth runway of this segment, as it is unknown for how many procedures and markets their technology is the best choice in the medical segment.

Another risk is the accelerated deterioration of the other two segments. Even if medical continues to grow strongly, further declines and losses in the other two segments could lead to worsening conditions for the consolidated company for years to come.

These risks are also what investors should look out for in the coming quarters. MTLS will be reporting earnings on May 7th; we are closely watching reported growth and profitability metrics for all segments. We are especially interested in more granular guidance when it comes to how exactly each segment is expected to perform over the full year and beyond.

Conclusion

Even though MTLS' software and manufacturing segments have shown considerable weakness in recent years, their medical segment alone more than makes up for it. As the medical segment continues to grow steadily, the other segments become increasingly irrelevant. When valuing the medical segment alone, our valuation target is much higher than where the stock currently trades at. However, this valuation target hinges on continued medical growth and stability in the other segments, leading to a "Buy" instead of a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: Lemonade: 'Buy' The Dip As IFP Soars
data.description:
Many investors are nervous about the S&P 500 sitting near all-time highs despite a wide variety of macroeconomic headwinds. We have to be aware of the fact, however, that most of those gains are concentrated in a tiny handful of semiconductor stocks. Much of the small and mid-cap space, especially in the tech sector, remains in a deep bear market blemished by fears of a "SaaSpocalypse."

I'm keen to go bargain hunting in this space, especially for compelling growth stories that have leveraged AI to improve internal processes and drive new business. Lemonade, Inc. (LMND), the digital-first insurance company, is a core buying opportunity in this regard. This is especially true after the stock fell nearly 10% after reporting very strong Q1 results, extending losses this year to ~20%.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Lemonade in February, when the stock was trading in the mid-$50s. I continue to see a very compelling buying opportunity here, especially as the company is ramping up new diversified insurance businesses (Pet and Car). Loss ratios are improving as the company's premium base scales, prompting me to reiterate my "Buy" rating on this company.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Lemonade, these are what I view to be the core bull case drivers for the company:

  • Lower reinsurance share is driving sharp revenue growth. Since July 2025, the company has reduced the share of its premiums that it's ceding to reinsurers from 55% to 20%, allowing the company to benefit from a far greater share of the customers that it's paying to acquire.
  • Line of sight to 2027 profitability. A growing premium base, improving loss ratios, and controlled opex are all positioning the company on a path to achieve its first full year of adjusted EBITDA profitability in FY27.
  • Positioning itself to become an insurance giant with multiple businesses. Though Lemonade began as a home/renters insurance product, it has now scaled its Pet business to become roughly equivalent. The company notes that its IFP (in-force premium) per employee has reached $1 million, which it describes as in parity with industry incumbents like GEICO and Allstate. We see Lemonade becoming one of the major insurance platforms of the future, with seamless digital quotes and a broad product platform to cross-sell, and significant international operations advantages with its use of AI to automate the claims process.

Stay long here: in my view, given very strong fundamentals, the post-earnings dip in Lemonade should be temporary.

Q1 Download

Let's now run through Lemonade's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

The core metric for Lemonade's business is in-force premium, essentially the premiums that Lemonade has earned in the period. IFP grew 32% y/y to $1.33 billion in the quarter. We note this is the tenth straight quarter of IFP acceleration for Lemonade, with growth rates improving slightly versus 31% y/y growth in Q4.

New products have been key to the company's success. Car insurance remains a very popular bundle for Lemonade, and as can be seen in the chart below, growth in the Car insurance vertical soared to 60% y/y.

At $214 million of IFP, Car now represents 16% of the company's total. The company notes that conversion rates are higher than other insurance products, reflecting attractive rates that Lemonade can offer (we note that in particular, Lemonade's partnership with Tesla is allowing the company to offer very attractive per-mile insurance policies for drivers who use Tesla's FSD). Per the company's commentary on Car growth in the Q1 shareholder letter:

From an underwriting perspective, we’re continuing to see encouraging trends across both new and renewal loss ratios over several quarters, even as growth has accelerated. Taken together, these trends reinforce our confidence in both the scalability and underlying economics of the Car business. While still early, we are encouraged by initial performance from our autonomous Car product. New customer conversion rates are ~70% higher than our comparable non-autonomous product, reflecting the compelling pricing we are able to offer customers. We expect to expand our autonomous Car product into multiple additional states this year.

The other core growth driver is Pet insurance, where IFP grew 56% y/y to $490 million and is now 37% of the company's total - just shy of its original home/renters' policies at $540 million in IFP.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the impacts of the company's lower reinsurance share, which kicked off in July 2025. In Q1, the company wrote $343.9 million in new insurance policies, of which $69.9 million (20%) was ceded to Lemonade's reinsurers. In the previous year's Q1, the company wrote $254.2 million in gross premiums but ceded 55% of that. As a result, though the company only wrote 35% y/y growth in new premiums, net new premiums are up 137% y/y.

This also helped to drive revenue growth of 71% y/y to $258.0 million, substantially beating Wall Street's expectations of $252.4 million (+67% y/y) by a four-point margin.

As Lemonade's premium base has grown and become more diversified, loss ratios have also improved. In Q1, gross loss ratios stood at 62%, representing a 16-point y/y improvement.

Strong results in Q1 also prompted Lemonade to boost its full-year outlook. The company is now expecting $1.632-$1.639 billion in IFP at year-end, or 32%-33% growth, versus $1.625-$1.630 billion expected previously.

The company is also still expecting adjusted EBITDA profits in Q4 this year and for the full year FY27.

Key Takeaways

Lemonade's Q1 results demonstrate a company that enjoys substantial tailwinds. Growth is accelerating across the company's various insurance verticals, which is leading to a very diversified premium base with improving loss ratios. AI-powered claims handling and efficient sales/marketing outlays are positioning the company for true profitability by next year. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: FMI International Equity - Unhedged Q1 2026 Review
data.description:
Investment Philosophy:

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Investment Process:

We utilize a business owner's approach to investing, thoroughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the characteristics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are responsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typically includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropriate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is discussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 2-4%. The portfolio generally consists of 25-40 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum.

International Unhedged (net) -7.31% -7.31% 5.84% 6.57% 3.12% - 3.91%

1 Estimated valuations are based on a representative account from the FMI International Equity Unhedged Composite. Weights and valuations for the strategy are based on fair value prices. Valuations are weighted average calculations, not reweighted to exclude cash, and financial companies are excluded from the EV/EBITDA calculation. Valuations for the portfolio are modified based on criteria identified by FMI. For more detailed information regarding these valuations, please contact FMI.

2 Source: Bloomberg - returns do not reflect management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Performance is based on market price returns. Beginning 8/10/20, market price returns are calculated using closing price. Prior to 8/10/20, market price returns were calculated using midpoint bid/ask spread at 4:00 PM ET.

Top 10 – Portfolio Holdings: 1

Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG) 6.4%
IMCD N.V. (IMCDY) 4.7%
Informa PLC (IFPJF) 4.7%
Sunbelt Rentals Holdings Inc. (SUNB) 4.3%
Sodexo S.A. (SDXOF) 4.1%
NOF Corp. (NOFCF) 4.0%
Fluidra S.A. (FLUIF) 3.9%
Ryanair Holdings PLC ADR (RYAAY) 3.9%
Techtronic Industries Co. Ltd. (TTNDY) 3.9%
Unilever PLC (UL) 3.7%

Portfolio Characteristics: 1

P/E Ratio (trailing one year) 23.8x
FY1 P/E Ratio (forward) 15.1x
FY2 P/E Ratio (forward) 13.5x
P/S Ratio 3.2x
P/B Ratio 4.0x
EV/EBITDA Ratio 10.7x
Number of Holdings 30
Active Share 96.3%

Recent Purchase:

Fluidra S.A. Ticker: FDR SM Added: September 2025

Fluidra is a leading pool equipment manufacturer and distributor. The company estimates that about half of its business comes from repair/replacement sales, around a third comes from outfitting newly built pools, and the remainder comes from pool remodels. Higher interest rates, soft housing markets, and weakening economies around the world have pressured discretionary pool spending. The trade war has also caused sentiment to sour because many of the products sold in the U.S. are manufactured in both Mexico and China. We believe Fluidra should be able to manage through tariffs by increasing price, negotiating with suppliers, and making changes to its supply chain. While we're not attempting to call a bottom in new pool construction, we believe the long-term outlook for this industry remains strong and the shares are attractively valued. We initiated a 1.7% position in the portfolio in September 2025.

Recent Sale:

ICON PLC Ticker: ICLR Sold: February 2026

ICON is a global contract research organization (CRO) that provides outsourced clinical trial design, enrollment, operations, and data validation services to pharmaceutical and biotech clients worldwide. The CRO industry continues to consolidate as trials are becoming more complex, patients are harder to find and enroll, and sponsors are ever mindful of the need for incremental efficiency. These development challenges play into the strengths of the largest CROs with capacity, expertise, and a global footprint. The pharmaceutical industry has been rationalizing research pipelines, causing industry growth to slow to a trickle. The original thesis was that we were buying the business during a period of cyclical pressure on pharmaceutical development spending, with the view that fundamentals would inflect higher as uncertainty eased. That recovery has been slower than originally anticipated and during the 1st quarter the company's audit committee announced an investigation into revenue recognition policies. After losing confidence in the firm's management team, we sold our 2.3% position in February 2026.

FMI was founded in 1980 and is an independent investment counseling firm registered with the SEC and the State of Wisconsin. The firm manages over $11.1 billion in assets of pension and profit sharing trusts, mutual funds, Taft-Hartley funds, insurance company portfolios, endowments and personal trusts. The firm includes both institutional and mutual fund business. Although the firm has participated in wrap programs, it is a separate and distinct business, and is excluded from firm-wide assets.

The International Equity Unhedged Composite was created and incepted on 12/31/2019. This composite invests mainly in a limited number (usually between 25-40) of large capitalization (namely, companies with more than $5 billion market capitalization) foreign companies. This strategy is not hedged to the United States Dollar.

The International Equity Unhedged Composite reflects time-weighted and asset-weighted returns for all discretionary accounts. All returns are calculated using United States Dollars and are based on monthly valuations using trade date accounting. All accounts in this composite are fee paying. Gross of fees returns are calculated gross of management fees, gross of custodial fees, gross of withholding taxes and net of transaction costs. Net of fees returns are calculated net of actual management fees and transaction costs and gross of custodial fees and withholding taxes. Dispersion is calculated using the equal weighted standard deviation of all accounts in the composite for the entire period. As of 12/31/2011, the trailing three year annualized ex-post standard deviation for the Composite and Benchmark are required to be stated per GIPS®. FMI uses gross returns to calculate these.

Currently, the advisory fee structure for the International Equity Unhedged Composite portfolios is as follows:

The minimum account sizes do not apply to new accounts for which there is a corporate, family, or other substantial relationship to existing accounts. In addition, the firm reserves the right to waive the minimum account size and minimum annual fee under certain circumstances. A complete list and description of all firm composites and FMI distributed mutual funds are available upon request.

The iShares MSCI EAFE ETF® seeks to track the investment results of the MSCI EAFE Index (the "Underlying Index"), which has been developed by MSCI Inc. (the "Index Provider" or "MSCI"). The Underlying Index is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure large- and mid-capitalization equity market performance of developed markets outside of the U.S. and Canada. The Underlying Index includes stocks from Europe, Australasia and the Far East and, as of July 31, 2021, consisted of securities from the following 21 developed market countries or regions: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland and the United Kingdom (the "U.K."). The MSCI EAFE Net Index (USD)® is a free float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed markets, excluding the US & Canada. The MSCI EAFE Net Index (USD)® consists of the following 21 developed market country indices: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom. It is reported in local currency and net of hedges. The International Equity Unhedged composite uses the iShares MSCI EAFE ETF® as its primary benchmark comparison. The benchmark is the iShares MSCI EAFE ETF®, which tracks the securities included in the MSCI EAFE Index®. The ETF returns are net of fees and reflect the deduction of all expenses and transaction costs incurred by the iShares MSCI EAFE ETF® and are net of withholding taxes. The iShares MSCI EAFE ETF® returns are based on net asset values (NAVs). As of December 31, 2025 the expense ratio was 0.32%. The iShares MSCI EAFE ETF® returns reflect official closing prices on the NYSE Arca.

GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein. Policies for valuing investments, calculating performance, and preparing GIPS Reports are available upon request.

Original Post

data.title: JPST: A Home For Your Cash
data.description:
The JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST) aims to earn current income while preserving capital. It goes about achieving this objective by holding investment-grade securities. The most current published data on the fund website shows around 60% of the portfolio being A and BBB rated securities.

There is also a decent amount in AAA-rated securities and close to 5% in US Treasury issuances. In terms of the types of investments held by this ETF, the summary prospectus provides the details.

.....corporate securities, asset-backed securities, mortgage-backed and mortgage-related securities, and high quality money market instruments such as commercial paper and certificates of deposit.

Source: Summary Prospectus

Additionally, JPST can also hold government securities of all shapes and forms, both domestic and foreign, as long as they are US dollar denominated. The holdings snapshot as of March 31 shows around two-thirds of the portfolio being invested in corporate securities and 4.7% invested in Treasuries.

The rest of the portfolio is in cash-like investments, like Certificates of Deposit, Commercial Paper and other cash equivalents, with only a small portion in asset-backed securities. So far we have seen that the JPST portfolio is comprised of high-quality [IG], liquid investments. Managing the credit and liquidity risk goes a long way in safeguarding capital, which is one of the goals of the ETF. Another factor to consider while preserving the principal of fixed income investments is the interest rate or duration risk. This is a measure of the extent to which the value of the security would decline with a 100 basis point increase in the interest rate and vice versa. With most of the portfolio maturing in under a year, JPST has a duration of 0.83 years.

A 100 basis points sudden increase in rates is not in the cards, but even if it were, the duration of the portfolio is not something JPST unitholders should lose sleep over. It is a non-issue. In fact, the duration risk at 0.83 years is even lower than the credit risk, which is denoted in the above graphic by the 0.99 years in spread duration. This metric gives a broad idea of the extent to which the portfolio would decline (less than a percent) should the credit spreads between Treasuries and the JPST portfolio were to widen by 100 basis points, and vice versa. For example, if the spreads were to widen by 100 basis points, you would see a jump in the yield to maturity, or YTM, number below, simply because the market value of the portfolio will decline by around 0.99%.

Practically speaking, the portfolio churn (66%) and the short-term maturities make the JPST portfolio well buttressed against duration, credit, and liquidity risk. The difference between the gross and net yield numbers that we see above is the annual expense ratio for this ETF, which is 0.18%.

This brings us to the other objective of this ETF, which is earning income for its unitholders. The JPST portfolio collectively yields around 4.26%, and the ETF has annual expenses of 0.18%. The two pieces of data give us an idea of the net income available to distribute to unitholders, which should be around 4.08%. JPST currently yields 4.10% (market price $50.57), which is close enough since the income and expenses are not necessarily spread evenly across the year.

Performance

JPST is an actively managed ETF, and as such, the advisor uses their discretion for strategies and asset mix based on market conditions.

In buying and selling investments for the Fund, the adviser looks for market sectors and individual securities that it believes will perform well over time. The adviser selects individual securities after performing a risk/reward analysis that includes an evaluation of their characteristics including income, interest rate risk, credit risk and the complex legal and technical structure of the transaction.

Source: Summary Prospectus

While there is no attempt at blindly replicating the performance of an Index like its passive counterparts, JPST considers the ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index a good representation of its "investment universe." Accordingly, the fund website shows JPST's performance alongside that of the index.

The fund has done better than the index over one year and longer time frames.

Verdict

If you are looking for "cash-like" returns with something that beats the index, JPST fits the bill. Of course "cash-like" is a loose term, and funds can and do take a lot of excess risk. JPST does tread that line fairly nicely, and a good metric to validate that would be the drop during COVID-19.

As you can see above, peak to trough during the "we are all gonna die!!!!!" phase was about 3%. We are pretty sure some of that was due to the ETF actually trading at a discount to its NAV. So all in, that is a fairly strong bunker performance. The current holdings give us no real cause for concern either. JPST comes in the middle of the pack relative to two other funds we follow in this "cash-like" category.

While iShares Short Duration Bond Active ETF (NEAR) has done better, it has done so while taking double the duration risk, and you can see that in the spike down of the yellow line during COVID-19. JPST remains a good choice for "cash-like" exposure. We, of course, prefer using an active approach with deep covered calls and shorter-term individual bonds, which often can give 3-6% alpha over JPST. Of course, that is not for everyone, and hence we write about such ETFs.

data.title: U.S. Solar Set To See A Significant Supply Chain Reconfiguration
data.description:
By Coco Zhang, ESG Research

Steep tariffs and strict foreign entities of concern (FEOC) rules under the Trump administration are accelerating a reset of the solar supply chain – on a compressed timeline. This poses challenges for developers that have long relied on China’s dominance in global production of cheap solar and battery equipment. Components with minimal Chinese exposure are likely to be in high demand, pushing up prices.

As developers rework their supply chains, short-term fixes may not be enough. More durable solutions will require building and maintaining trade relationships that can withstand future policy shifts.

Over time, a vertically integrated domestic supply chain is achievable, though it will require incentives and trade protections. The significant volume of solar cell capacity currently under construction in the US suggests progress in the right direction.

Seemingly high US module capacity does not ensure self-sufficiency

US solar manufacturing capacity has expanded rapidly. The Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA) estimates that as of early 2026, US solar module manufacturing capacity increased 50% annually to 65GW, much higher than the domestic demand of roughly 40 GW in 2025.

While we think this is a credible and impressive data point, we caution against the interpretation that the US is self-sufficient on solar modules. In fact, the US still imported 32GW of modules last year, despite having 65GW of module capacity targeting mostly domestic customers.

One main reason behind this is upstream manufacturing bottlenecks. Further up the supply chain of crystalline-silicon (c-Si) modules, cell capacity is just 3.2 GW, while wafer and ingot capacity are each around 5 GW. Therefore, US module capacity is not true ‘manufacturing capacity’, but largely ‘assembly capacity’ using imported cells.

This creates additional risks. First, module manufacturers may need to produce way below nameplate capacity if they cannot secure inputs on time and at competitive prices. Second, reliance on imported cells exposes module manufacturers, and by extension solar developers, to tariffs and FEOC restrictions. By contrast, cadmium telluride modules, led by First Solar, benefit from about 14GW of fully integrated domestic capacity.

Another reason the US remains reliant on imports is price competitiveness. Even after accounting for tariffs, imported solar modules, a large share of which has ties to China, remain cheaper than US-made modules. It is also worth noting that even imported solar modules are over twice as expensive as the global average, where tariffs are minimal, suggesting the US manufacturing industry is far from competitive in the global market.

These factors show that the US is far from fully localising its solar supply chain, though the 26GW of cell capacity under construction could improve the situation.

In the near term, however, many US projects will still rely on imports and be exposed to two policy risks: tariffs and FEOC requirements.

Tariffs continue to push China to find new trade reroutes

To curb tariff circumvention by China, the US sharply increased countervailing and anti-dumping duties on solar imports from Cambodia, Thailand, Malaysia, and Vietnam in early 2025. This prompted China to react. At the time, we noted in a research article that alternative trade paths were emerging in Laos and Indonesia. Now, not only has 2025 trade data confirmed this move, but it has shown how fast rerouting happened.

From 2024 to 2025, the combined import share of modules from Laos and Indonesia jumped from 3% to 40%, with the cell import share from these two countries surging from 5% to 53%. In contrast, the combined import share from Cambodia, Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam collapsed.

This rapid shift triggered a new US investigation. In February 2026, the Department of Commerce announced preliminary countervailing duties of 80.67%-143.3% on solar imports from Indonesia and Laos. It also imposed a 125% tariff on Indian imports, citing evidence of Chinese transshipment.

China’s manufacturing overcapacity will incentivise it to continue finding new trade routes. But doing so could become harder, as China’s remaining options in Southeast Asia are limited to markets such as the Philippines. And there is potentially Africa. Recent reports suggest solar manufacturing in Africa is expanding, although it is unclear how much is influenced by China.

Looking ahead, there are more trade issues we are watching:

  • Court ruling on previous Southeast Asia tariff exemption: In August 2025, the US Court of International Trade struck down a Biden-era rule that exempted for two years hefty tariffs on solar imports from Cambodia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam. The decision is now under appeal. If upheld, it could result in over $50bn in retroactive duties, dealing a major blow to US solar.
  • First Solar’s patent lawsuit: In March, the US International Trade Commission launched an investigation into whether several solar manufacturers – including Canadian Solar and Jinko Solar – are infringing First Solar’s TOPCon (Tunnel Oxide Passivated Contact) patent. The case could take up to 18 months, but a ruling in First Solar’s favour could significantly constrain solar equipment supply.
  • Appetite to shift toward non-China-linked suppliers: There has been little increase in US imports from non-China-linked alternatives, such as South Korea, despite tariffs on Southeast Asian countries. It is worth seeing if that limited appetite can rise this year.

As developers map and rework their supply chains, they should focus not only on short-term fixes but also on durable solutions. For manufacturers, this means building partnerships up and down the supply chain to offer developers a more integrated model. That is the approach taken by Corning, Suniva, and Heliene, which together are establishing US-made polysilicon, wafers, cells, and modules.

FEOC guidance prompts supply chain reevaluation – it’s all about proving yourself

Another major policy risk stems from FEOC rules, which are designed to limit Chinese influence. FEOC compliance has two parts: developers cannot be prohibited foreign entities (PFEs), and they cannot rely on material assistance from such entities. Starting in 2026, companies with PFE ties will no longer qualify for clean-energy tax credits.

In February, the Treasury Department issued draft guidance on material assistance. The key point is that solar and BESS developers only need to review a specific list of upstream components and ensure they are free of FEOC influence. The guidance adds tremendous certainty for developers to manage supply chains and advance projects.

However, in practice, ensuring FEOC compliance is not easy. Making sure PV module manufacturers are not PFEs could already be challenging. BloombergNEF estimates that over 20% of US solar module capacity may have high FEOC exposure through corporate structures or IP partnerships. That could be enough to fail the FEOC test.

Manufacturers that pass ownership and IP tests can still be disqualified if they receive material assistance from PFEs. Here, ensuring cells are compliant is the most critical step, as they account for the largest share of equipment costs used to assess FEOC status. But with limited compliant supply, this step can be challenging for some developers.

And full clarity will not come until the Treasury releases further guidance later this year to define PFEs more precisely and tighten rules against circumvention. A tougher stance means that Chinese firms might not be able to comply simply through restructuring ownership, financing, or contracts.

Now, what developers need to do is not just verify whether they are FEOC compliant, but show robust proof of how they are compliant. To that end, supplier certifications do provide a certain level of comfort, as they are legally binding documents with penalties of perjury. But lenders are increasingly expecting more intense audits or third-party diligence. Detailed documentation is becoming critical to securing project financing.

Conclusion

Solar reshoring across the value chain is still progressing slowly, though momentum is building. Continued reliance on a China-dominated global market, combined with tariffs and FEOC rules, means solar developers need significant work to make their supply chains bulletproof.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: MongoDB: Strong Platform, Uncertain Inflection, Watching The Transition
data.description:
MongoDB, Inc. (MDB) positions itself as a high-quality, developer-focused database platform that has undergone many years of high revenue growth despite not being able to turn a GAAP profit yet. The story is in its transition to becoming a profitable, AI-era infrastructure company through its cloud database engine and native vector search offerings. The stock trades at a 44x forward adjusted P/E, which we believe is deserved owing to its high revenue visibility. But the stock has taken a beating from its YTD highs with a ~38% decline. We think risks are balanced, but more proof regarding uptake among its AI-native customers is needed to justify further appreciation.

Financial Analysis

MDB's revenue growth rate has noticeably stabilized around ~20% Y/Y during FY2025 to FY2026 from the 30% to 40% Y/Y growth rates experienced during FY2021 to FY2024 (the company's year-end is in January). Its EBITDA and GAAP net margins have improved substantially over the past few years despite being negative. Its adjusted EBITDA and net income figures have followed a similar accelerating trend but are positive due to the adjustment of stock-based compensation expenses, which accounted for ~22% of revenue in FY2026, improving from ~31% in FY2022.

Margin has steadily improved primarily due to relatively fixed infrastructure costs and improving operating expense efficiency.

We project the company to grow revenue in the high teens on its robust Atlas engine offering while benefiting from its AI positioning. Operating leverage should continue to improve, with GAAP profitability achievable by FY2028E.

Metric FY2025A FY2026A FY2027E FY2028E FY2029E
Revenue $2,006 million $2,464 million $2,896 million $3,394 million $4,022 million
Revenue Growth YoY 19.2% 22.8% 17.6% 17.2% 18.5%
Adj. Gross Margin 76.0% 74.0% 74.2% 74.4% 74.8%
Adj. EBITDA $311 million $479 million $576 million $721 million $920 million
Adj. EBITDA Margin 15.5% 19.4% 19.9% 21.2% 22.9%
Adj. EPS $3.66 $4.97 $5.84 $7.05 $8.64
Adj. EPS Growth 9.9% 35.8% 17.4% 20.7% 22.6%
FCF $114 million $493 million $524 million $652 million $881 million
Net Debt / (Cash) ($2,337 million) ($2,387 million) ($2,793 million) ($3,439 million) ($4,712 million)

Source: Company, Market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates.

Atlas Engine, The Core Growth Driver

MDB's defining product is its Atlas engine, which has helped the company grow from $65 million in revenue in FY2016 to $2.5 billion in FY2026.

In FY2026, Atlas' total customers have surpassed 65 thousand, with ~2.8 thousand contributing over $100 thousand in ARR, up meaningfully from 2,052 from two years ago, according to CFO Michael Berry on the Q4 FY2026 earnings call. Its customer base has grown ~20% annually, in line with its revenue growth rate.

An advantage over seat-based SaaS is that Atlas revenues scale with increasing workloads without having to wait for a new sales cycle for existing customers to upgrade. Thus, the net ARR expansion rate has recovered to 121% in Q4 FY2026.

Further, MongoDB has signed its largest total contract value deal of over $100 million with a financial institution and another $90 million deal with a technology firm in Q4 FY2026. In our view, these landmark deals help solidify Atlas's position as a first-tier enterprise product that is gaining momentum.

Remaining performance obligations (RPO) surged 97% Y/Y to $1.47 billion in Q4 FY2026, predicting accelerating future revenue under contract and bolstering revenue visibility.

Benefiting From AI's Structural Tailwind

MDB is integrating closely into enterprise AI stacks through the database layer, which is foundational for any AI application. As such, this is an important medium-term opportunity, as MDB has moved to become the unified operational plus retrieval data store.

Atlas natively embeds vector search alongside operational data, which eliminates the need for a separate specialized vector database. This unified approach allows for the reduction in latency and integration complexity and lowers the total cost of ownership.

MongoDB acquired Voyage AI in February 2025, with the latter being a pioneer in advanced embedding and reranking models. The integration allows a native automated embedding generation in Atlas Vector Search. The developer simply sets up a vector search index, and the embedding creation is automatically handled behind the scenes with Voyage AI models.

The company benefits from the agentic AI tailwind—these agents need to retrieve real-time and contextualized data from a document-oriented database with native vector search and governance enabled. As such, MongoDB is positioning itself well as the data foundation for the agentic AI era.

During the Q4 call, the management was optimistic regarding how AI will drive upside for the company, as they have seen encouraging trends from their subset of AI-native customers. However, it appeared that it was still rather early in their adoption of MDB products, and they were not yet a meaningful driver of revenue. This is something investors can keep an eye out for.

Key Risks

Near-term softness: FY2027 was guided to a revenue deceleration of 16% to 18% Y/Y growth (implied from the $2.86 billion to $2.90 billion guide) from FY26's 22.8% Y/Y growth rate. We believe that the management is cautious, particularly on the timing of revenue recognition in multi-year deals.

Ongoing leadership transition: simultaneous changes in the C-suite, including the CEO, CFO, and CRO, in the recent year add to meaningful execution risk for an enterprise-heavy business.

Competition: Large hyperscalers have developed similar, compatible alternatives like Amazon.com, Inc.'s (AMZN) DocumentDB, Microsoft Corporation's (MSFT) Azure Cosmos DB, or Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) Google Firestone, which will present formidable, subsidized competition.

AI revenue remains nascent: despite encouraging qualitative guidance from the management, it has been alluded that AI-native customers are yet to be a meaningful driver. Uptake from this subset will be key in the coming quarters.

Valuation And Verdict

The structural investment case for MDB has been that Atlas has been a well-received product among developers while being a widely embedded database platform with an accelerating AI layer. Financially, the company is projected to deliver revenue growth of ~20% Y/Y with expanding margins.

It trades at a forward adjusted earnings of 44x, which is reasonable due to the high visibility of its future revenue stream. However, the company does face headwinds as the new leadership team is untested, while near-term guidance is softer than market expectations.

In our view, we rate MDB a Hold, as we think the stock is fairly valued until we see more evidence that their AI-native customers are increasing their spend with them.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: EPR Properties, Petrobras, Navitas And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between April 17 and April 23.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

EPR Properties: My Top 'Must-Buy' REIT For 2026

Rida Morwa | Buy

Life has a way of derailing plans. Have you ever planned a major event, like a wedding, birthday party, or corporate retreat, and then, as the event nears, things start going wrong? The power goes out at the venue, a storm comes in, the decorations are late, the caterer gets lost, and I thought YOU were going to pick up the cake!

It's just a reality of life that people can't predict all the things that will go wrong in the future. Of course, in the future, we'll be looking back and stating that it was obvious what was going to happen.

In three months, everyone will be a genius about what trades you should have made three months ago. People will be proclaiming that they “knew” that the Iran war was going to escalate and oil was going to go up to $200/barrel, or that they “knew” a peace deal would be reached and that the Strait of Hormuz would be open for business, and oil is headed back to $70/barrel.

We forget how quickly the world can change in just three months. Remember January 2020? REITs were soaring high, the stock market was strong, and investors were optimistic about the future. The talking heads on TV were shouting, “Buy, Buy, Buy!”

Three months later, the sky was falling, COVID-19 was shutting everything down, and the mayhem and economic damage were going to be unprecedented. “Sell! Sell! Sell!”

Today, we want to take a closer look at a REIT that had its plans materially derailed by COVID-19. It had just sold a large portion of its portfolio and was looking to deploy capital into properties where people congregated in large groups.

Read more here.

PDI: 15% Yield That Never Keeps Me Up At Night

Roberts Berzins, CFA | Buy

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) is one of the highest-yielding PIMCO closed-end-fund (or CEF) products out there.

Currently, it offers a dividend yield of 15.3%, which is slightly above the historical average. The yield has jumped this high not because of distribution hikes but because of price declines. ...

Over the past 6-month period, PDI's unit price has decreased by ~8%, which is largely attributable to the rising long-term yield.

Given the ~15% yield, recent price declines, and, importantly, what has been happening in the economy, PDI might seem too risky for prudent income investors.

And I totally get it.

On the surface, PDI does indeed look like a classical value trap: e.g., "too good to be true" yield, negative price correction when the volatility increases, and the underlying fundamentals that are associated with non-investment-grade securities.

In fact, I had a similar perspective back in October 2025, when I did a deep-dive assessment of PDI and its fundamentals: PDI: I Tried To Plot A Bear Case, I Failed.

As the title of the article indicates, I arrived at a different conclusion, a more bullish conclusion. Since the publication of this article, PDI's unit price has declined by ~6%, but on a total return basis, the investment is slightly up.

So, today PDI has a higher yield and smaller premium (~7% over NAV) compared to the moment when I circulated my previous piece.

And, obviously, there have been some notable changes in the macro environment or the so-called structural financial dynamics.

Read more here.

The Market Has Totally Misread The Petrobras Opportunity

Robert & Sam Kovacs | Strong Buy

During the past two months, I've covered the conflict between Iran and the US extensively, publishing 7 deep dives on Seeking Alpha, updating readers on the situation and how it changed reality for the economy moving forward.

Amid all of this analysis, one stock that I previously had sidestepped emerged as an ever more convincing pick: Petrobras (PBR).

A convergence of events happened, making this a natural pick.

Read more here.

Navitas Is One Misstep Away From A Sharp Re-Rating

Summit Research | Hold

Navitas Semiconductor (NVTS) stock has jumped more than 545% since announcing its non-exclusive collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA) to support power solutions for the upcoming 800V High Voltage Data Center (“800V HVDC”) architecture beginning in 2027. This momentum has been reinforced by the Navitas 2.0 pivot under new CEO Chris Allexandre’s leadership since August, as the company onboards a transition to higher-margin, high-power markets spanning AI data centers, grid and energy infrastructure, performance computing, and industrial electrification. Just this past Thursday (April 16), the stock appreciated close to 20% on no particular news, underscoring market momentum in Navitas' underlying AI power narrative trade.

Yet the widening disconnect between the stock’s premium and the underlying business’ fundamentals reflects the market’s view of the company as a critical provider of power solutions for next-generation AI data center architectures. Specifically, Navitas is currently viewed as one of the few pure-play providers of gallium nitride (“GaN”) power solutions needed in the impending transition to high-power 800V HVDCs in 2027 when Nvidia deploys its next-generation Kyber racks builds for the Vera Rubin Ultra architecture. This opportunity is further compounded by the strategic alignment of Navitas’ high- and ultra-voltage SiC portfolio with upcoming U.S. grid infrastructure upgrades catalyzed by increasingly demanding AI-driven power requirements.

However, despite Navitas’ growing strategic relevance in scaling next-generation AI infrastructure deployments, it has yet to demonstrate credible visibility into an earnings inflection at scale. Admittedly, management has deliberately reduced Navitas’ mix of lower-margin mobile revenues in recent quarters and has instead been prioritizing higher-margin high-power GaN and SiC solution sales that target the AI data center, grid and energy infrastructure, performance computing, and industrial electrification end-markets. But even as revenue mix improves, Navitas remains exposed to an elevated fixed cost structure, underscoring prolonged uncertainty around the timing and durability of earnings expansion at scale.

Taken together, I believe the stock is becoming increasingly untethered from its underlying fundamentals. Even if Navitas’ upcoming Q1 earnings update outperforms expectations, the result is unlikely to be a needle-mover in supporting further upside, given limited scale to absorb its currently elevated cost base. Instead, upside will likely remain driven primarily by factors that reinforce narrative—such as the increasingly mission-critical role of power tech advancements for AI applications—rather than tangible fundamentals. This accordingly creates a volatile setup for a stock, with downside protection from real thematic relevance but upside constrained by a premium that appears increasingly speculative and lacking tangible fundamental support.

Read more here.

Capital Southwest: Thriving While The BDC Market Suffers

Cain Lee | Buy

When I previously covered Capital Southwest (CSWC), I upgraded my rating to a buy following the strength of the earnings report at the time. Since that last coverage, CSWC has experienced an upside rally, and the total return outpaces that of peer BDCs and the S&P 500 over the same time frame. While the rest of the BDC sector continues to face headwinds, CSWC has been able to thrive while other BDCs are simply trying to survive. Therefore, I wanted to revisit CSWC to assess its current strengths and weaknesses as it approaches the next earnings report. I believe that CSWC is one of the best choices within the BDC sector, but quality tends to be expensive.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CSWC's share price has increased by nearly 23.5%. While the rest of the BDC sector faces headwinds, CSWC continues to demonstrate its resilience. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to nearly 40% over the same time frame. CSWC now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 10.5% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. CSWC has demonstrated that it has no issues generating earnings that exceed the distributions being paid out to shareholders.

CSWC currently trades at the higher end of its price-to-NAV range, making valuation a bit tricky. If you are optimistic about the outlook of future interest rate cuts, CSWCC may improve its performance. However, this depends on the portfolio's ability to allocate capital to new investments that can continue to increase its underlying NAV. The fund's NAV has increased over the last year, making CSWC a standout performer among peers. While there are some risks to be considered, I believe that CSWC is one of the strongest opportunities in the market right now.

Read more here.

MercadoLibre Has Gone Nowhere For 5 Years, That Can Change Soon

StockBros Research | Buy

MercadoLibre (MELI) is one of, if not the best, Latin American companies out there. Yet, the stock has only returned around 19% in the past five years after huge gains in the five years before that. But that's the opportunity.

The valuation has now caught up to the growth potential, with a forward PEG ratio not too far away from 1x. Some investors might be worried about the company's investments in growth, which have hurt margins a bit, but I'd argue that MercadoLibre is making the right move. And it's doing what it always does, investing in the future to capture more profits over time. Analysts expect margins to recover next year and in 2028, which can propel the stock higher relatively soon.

Additionally, it's a leader in Latin American e-commerce by far, and its market share is expected to get bigger over time, all while EPS growth is forecast to be in the 28-40% range for the next three years.

For these reasons, I give MELI stock a Buy rating.

Read more here.

NASA: This New ETF Owns SpaceX And Could Lift Off

Ian Bezek | Buy

Traders are gravitating to space stocks once again. With the SpaceX IPO expected to launch later this year, a lot of money is heading into the sector. And understandably so. SpaceX looks like it will be one of the largest IPOs of all time.

Furthermore, SpaceX is likely to get added to major indexes shortly after its trading debut. That, in turn, should cause more passive funds to move into SpaceX stock and potentially the space industry more broadly. That makes it time to highlight a new space ETF with a SpaceX angle. ...

I want to be clear, from the jump, that valuations for both SpaceX and the broader space sector seem quite elevated at the moment. From a fundamental standpoint, there is significant risk across the subset of space stocks. However, from a structural perspective, I think the Tema Space Innovators ETF (NASA) has some unique attributes that the broader investment community hasn't become fully aware of yet. This explains my bullish view on NASA, at least for the next six months to a year.

Read more here.

British American Tobacco: Why This 6% Yield Remains My Largest Holding

Philipp Brohl | Buy

It has been a while since I last covered my largest holding by far, British American Tobacco (BTI), to which I'm going to refer as BAT going forward.

For those who didn't follow my previous coverage, I bought the stock at the height of the tobacco bear market a couple of years ago, when the shares offered close to double-digit dividend yields. With the rising share price and valuation, I have downgraded the rating from "Strong Buy" to "Buy".

Momentum investors might disagree with me here, but in my opinion, that's the right approach, especially when it comes to low-growth businesses like tobacco companies. We have to be bullish when prices are low and dividend yields are high, and we become more cautious once the valuation rises.

Whenever investing in "boring" consumer staples, the dividend is a major factor in total returns over the very long term. Naturally, we want our starting yield to be high and the valuation to be low. That way, we not only profit from juicy dividend yields but also from potential multiple expansion.

And that's exactly what happened with British American Tobacco. Total returns have been staggering from its bear market lows, even outpacing the NASDAQ...

But the past is the past, so where do we stand now?

Read more here.

ATI Inc.: Why I Remain Bullish On Aerospace And Defense Growth

Dhierin Bechai | Strong Buy

In January, I marked ATI Inc. (ATI) a strong buy with a $163.13 bullish price target. Since then, the stock has reached my price target. That may be somewhat remarkable given the market turmoil over the past weeks and aerospace and defense names definitely not being favored by the market. With the price targets reached and earnings coming up later this month, it is a good moment to revisit ATI Inc.

Read more here.

Brookfield: Finding The Right Mix Of Capital Growth, Hard Assets, And High Margins

Elizabeth Pramila | Buy

The Brookfield opportunity comes in many forms, but at the end of the day it is all about efficient capital deployment to achieve desired results. Viewed through that lens, one's decision to invest in one or another part of the Brookfield franchise is better informed with a sound understanding of the underlying entities and operations, and how that impacts one's relationship to this closely-knit group of businesses.

To be precise, choosing the right Brookfield vehicle means knowing what one is investing in so one can decide the manner in which one receives any returns on the investment. It also allows one to control the level of risk one perceives. With that context front and center, I shall try to demonstrate how various Brookfield opportunities can play very dynamic roles alongside a core investment in the parent entity.

Read more here.

IWMI: A Different Take On Income Investing

Gustavo Ribeiro | Buy

I rate the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) as a Buy. The main reason is simple. It offers something that’s still hard to find in the options income ETF space: real exposure to small-cap stocks, paired with a strategy that generates income without completely giving up growth potential.

Most high-income ETFs stick to large-cap indexes like the S&P 500 or Nasdaq-100. Many funds end up looking like slight variations of the same idea, especially with mega-cap stocks dominating those indexes, with the MAG 7 representing over 30% of the S&P 500. IWMI stands out because it focuses on the Russell 2000, where volatility runs higher and option premiums tend to be richer. That creates a stronger foundation for income while adding exposure to a part of the market that often gets overlooked.

This isn’t only about yield. IWMI gives investors a way to own small caps while getting paid for the added volatility. For anyone trying to boost income without piling even more into mega-cap-heavy strategies, that’s a meaningful difference.

Read more here.

Grab: The Market Got This One Wrong

Jamelle Danne Rosales | Strong Buy

Let me start this article off by saying that I may sound biased because I'm a first-hand customer/consumer of Grab's (GRAB) products and services, but this doesn't change the fact that this is a good company, not only boasting profits and achieving management targets, but I can see, first-hand, its positive effects of giving people jobs and merchants to participate in a competitive market, which is why I think it's a very interesting buy.

It's commonly known for ride-hailing and food delivery services, but it has embedded itself in the ecosystem that they now also offer financial services.

Another part that makes me more positive on Grab is the strength of its user base. Like what I've said above, as a consumer, Grab really is a good platform, but it's also cool to see strong activity and growth in transaction volume across other Southeast Asian countries, which tells me people are using the app more often, not just spending more per order.

GrabFin is another reason I'm leaning bullish since they're able to really embed themselves in the ecosystem by being more involved in every consumer's way of life. Need food or groceries? Deliveries? And this time, do you need a loan? Grab can finally offer that to you.

Naturally, their financial services segment still has room to grow, and if it reaches breakeven as management expects, that could become a major margin driver for the whole company.

I also think the balance sheet gives Grab some breathing room, especially in a time where oil prices have significantly increased in SEA countries and a probable reduction of consumer spending. Having a strong cash position lowers the downside and gives the company flexibility to keep investing in growth, buybacks, and new opportunities.

That is why I do not believe that the stock is risk-free, because things like regulation in Indonesia, competitive food delivery, and even fuel cost pressure are all real things that may slow the story and, overall, slow down the company's growth.

In the end, I think that Grab is a Strong Buy. I believe that the market might continue to underestimate the combination of improving profitability, and financial services upside as well as the underpinning of its cash position. The thesis, in my case, can be reduced to the following: Grab is beginning to resemble a true operating business with a variety of growth drivers rather than a high-tech narrative with high beta.

Read more here.


Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Blackbaud: 2026 Renewals Will Be The Major Test (Upgrade)
data.description:
We've entered the thick of the Q1 earnings season, and all eyes are firmly on the software sector, where the fears of a "SaaSpocalypse" have cratered many software stocks to multi-year lows. My overall macro view remains that AI will impact software companies in different ways, and we'll really have to parse through each company's pricing model and its product stack before declaring that it is truly at risk of disruption.

Blackbaud, Inc. (BLKB), a vertical software company aimed at non-profits, rose ~7% after reporting slightly better than expected Q1 results. Still, the stock remains down ~30% since the start of January and nearly 40% over the past year. Now trading at a rare single-digit P/E, we have to ask the key question: Is Blackbaud set to rebound, or has it permanently re-rated downward?

I last wrote a "Sell" article on Blackbaud in February, when the stock was trading near $48 per share. Since then, two positives have moved in Blackbaud's favor. First, the company delivered better-than-expected Q1 results, including stabilization in the company's organic revenue growth rates, which had been decelerating for several quarters. Second, Blackbaud's own valuation has compressed to much more appealing levels that help to compensate for some of the company's operating risks. I'm boosting my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for this name. On the positive side for Blackbaud:

  • A chance to grow revenue through renewals in 2026. The company's cohort of customers up for renewal is substantially larger in 2026 versus 2025. A key growth driver for the company over the past several years has been to push price increases upon renewal, giving Blackbaud a chance to accelerate its growth this year as long as renewal rates remain steady or improve.
  • The customer base is unlikely to deploy agentic AI to replace Blackbaud's technology. Blackbaud serves primarily non-profits, and its solutions are far-ranging: from grant management, donations tracking, CRM, and payments processing. We find it unlikely that a large chunk of Blackbaud's customers have the capacity or willingness to replace Blackbaud products with in-house developed AI solutions.

At the same time, however, we have to note the multiple risks stacked against the company:

  • Churn could increase in FY26. Amid the higher renewal base also comes the chance for greater churn. Especially in a tougher macroeconomy where donations may dry up, we could see more customers head for the exits.
  • Substantial debt pile. Unlike many software companies, Blackbaud is heavily indebted, with over $1 billion in net debt on its latest balance sheet. The company has a very limited, ~$34 million cash balance, which doesn't give a lot of financial flexibility for a company that has historically been very reliant on M&A to drive growth.

Ultimately, however, what makes me a bit more optimistic on Blackbaud's prospects is price. At current share prices near $40, Blackbaud trades at a market cap of just $1.82 billion. Netting off the $34 million of cash and $1.19 billion of debt on Blackbaud's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $2.98 billion.

As shown in the chart below, for FY26 Blackbaud has sustained its original guidance, which calls for ~4% revenue growth, $434 million in midpoint adjusted EBITDA, and $285 million in free cash flow.

This positions the company's valuations at:

  • 6.9x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.
  • 10.5x EV/FY26 FCF.
  • 7.7x FY26 P/E (that said, I view P/E to be a misleading metric for Blackbaud given its debt load).

In my view, a ~10x FCF multiple fairly values this company relative to its mid-single-digit growth prospects. If Blackbaud shares positive news on renewal trends for its 2026 renewal cohort, we could see a further upward re-rating in the stock to unwind some of this year's losses. With this possibility on the horizon, I'm adding Blackbaud back to my watch list - but don't rush in to buy just yet.

Q1 Download

Let's now go through Blackbaud's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Blackbaud's revenue grew 4.2% y/y to $281.1 million, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $280.1 million (+3.8% y/y) by a half-point margin. Revenue accelerated meaningfully relative to a 2.3% decline in Q4, which we note was encumbered by a tough compare after the company divested its underperforming edtech subsidiary, EverFi.

Q1 is the first quarter in which organic growth matches as-reported growth. In Q1, the company notched 4.2% organic growth, largely driven by recurring revenue from both software subscriptions and payment processing. This growth rate was stable versus 4.3% growth in Q4, which is another reason to be relieved after multiple quarters of organic growth deceleration.

Of course, the true test lies in this year's renewals. As shown in the chart below, the company notes that its FY26 renewal cohort is ~40% greater than last year. As previously mentioned, this comes with both risk and opportunity. Blackbaud typically increases pricing and revenue on its renewal deals, but renewal behavior may be different this year, especially with many organizations reconsidering their tech stacks in the wake of AI.

We note that Blackbaud's guidance already includes a 50-75 bps negative impact from renewals, driven by expected churn with renewal rates forecasted in line with prior years.

Additional good news is the fact that Blackbaud notes it is gravitating toward longer contract terms, which is an indication that customers trust Blackbaud and aren't preparing to rip it out in favor of AI anytime soon. Per CEO Mike Gianoni's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We win because our solutions are intuitive, require fewer complex customizations and integrations and translate advances like AI into practical outcomes customers can trust, building confidence that is supporting longer contract terms at renewal.

As I mentioned last quarter, over 20% of our customers are on 4-year or longer contract terms. This quarter, we continue to see a nice mix of new customer logo wins and selling additional solutions to our existing customers. Some examples of new logos were competitive displacements across many of our verticals.

We note as well that internally, Blackbaud notes it has begun to leverage AI to automate certain workflows, such as writing software code as well as helping sales teams qualify inbound leads. We are hopeful that these actions will lead to more controlled headcount growth. Blackbaud's pro forma operating margins in Q1 rose 120 bps y/y to 29.6%:

Blackbaud's pro forma EPS of $1.14 also grew 20% y/y (ahead of the company's 17% EPS growth target for the year) and beat Street expectations of $1.12 by 2%.

Key Takeaways

With stabilized organic revenue and a chance to outperform expectations with the larger renewal cohort this year, Blackbaud has a more compelling risk/reward profile now, especially at a ~10x FCF multiple. Keep a close eye on this stock as it progresses through the year: I'm not comfortable investing in this stock for the long haul until we get a glimpse at renewal trends, which should offer an indication of how the macro and AI are playing into customer retention decisions.

data.title: Euro And ECB
data.description:
The euro fell nearly 1.65% in Q1, only to claw back about 1.25% in April. At one level, that looks like noise within a broader range. At another, it reflects a market that has been repeatedly and abruptly forced to reprice the policy outlook in response to shifting geopolitical and macroeconomic crosscurrents.

The ECB meets tomorrow, and a hawkish hold remains the most likely scenario. There is roughly a 10% chance of a hike priced in for this meeting. June is fully discounted for a quarter-point move. That asymmetry alone tells you something important: the bar to action tomorrow is high, but the bar to signaling is not.

The evolution of rate expectations over the past several weeks has been nothing short of dramatic. Before the escalation of the Middle East conflict, the market was still leaning toward another cut this year - about a 55% probability. Then came a sharp, violent reversal. At the peak of the repricing on April 7, three hikes were fully discounted and the market was flirting with a fourth. This seemed exaggerated, but even now, the swaps market has three hikes fully priced.

Energy prices have been the most direct transmission channel. July Brent pushed to a new contract high, $108-109 today. Even when hostilities end, the supply risk premium is unlikely to vanish quickly. This matters for Europe more than most. The region remains acutely sensitive to imported energy costs, and the second-round effects - while not yet evident in the data - are a key risk the ECB cannot dismiss.

The ECB's own survey data bear this out. Inflation expectations are rising. That explains why the market has been willing to pivot so aggressively from pricing cuts to pricing hikes. It also explains why the ECB is unlikely to push back too forcefully against current market pricing. A central bank that spent the better part of two years trying to anchor expectations is not eager to see them drift again.

The incoming data ahead of tomorrow's decision is notable. Q1 GDP is expected around 0.2%, matching Q4's pace - not recessionary, but hardly reassuring. April's preliminary CPI is expected to rise to around 3.0% from 2.6%, with a monthly gain close to 1.0% following March's 1.3%. Much of the impulse traces to energy and food; core appears broadly stable. In other words, the inflation story is visible but has not yet broadened enough to force an immediate response.

The labor market remains a source of quiet support for the hawks. The unemployment rate is expected near 6.2%, effectively matching the lowest levels of the EMU era. That gives the ECB room to maintain a tightening bias even as growth stays subdued.

From a market perspective, the euro's trajectory remains tethered to rate differentials. The US two-year premium over Germany - a relationship that tracks the exchange rate closely - narrowed sharply this year, from just above 150 basis points in mid-January to just over 100 earlier this month, the tightest in three years. It widened back toward 130, but has since compressed again to around 118. For context, it sat near 200 basis points a year ago. That compression has been one of the euro's most reliable supports. That said, the end of the Middle East war would likely see a risk-on move by investors that would buoy the euro.

The technical picture, however, has turned more cautious. The euro peaked near $1.1850 on April 17 and has since reversed lower. The initial target in the $1.1675-1.1700 area was met, but selling pressure did not look exhausted. Momentum indicators point to scope for additional losses. The next area of interest is around $1.1650.

Volatility, meanwhile, has been retreating. Three-month implied euro vol slipped to a three-month low earlier this week, just below 5.8%, against a 50-day moving average near 6.7% and a 100-day around 6.3%. The compression suggests the market is less concerned about large directional moves and more comfortable expressing views through carry and relative rates. Still, we suspect that implied vol is poised to move higher.

Finally, the consensus has quietly turned a shade less bullish. Bloomberg's latest survey puts the euro at $1.1750 at end-June and $1.19 by end-September and year-end - both a touch lower than a month ago. That shift may appear marginal, but it reflects a wider recognition that the path higher is unlikely to be linear.

The near term hinges heavily on how the ECB handles tomorrow. A hawkish hold would validate much of the recent repricing without committing to immediate action. That may be enough - for now. But with energy prices elevated, inflation expectations on the move, and geopolitical risks unresolved, the balance of risks stays fluid. The euro's recovery is real. It is not yet secure.

Original Post

data.title: Eli Lilly Stock Could Rise Sharply After Earnings
data.description:
Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) has seen its stock fall by more than 20% since peaking in mid-January and has not even participated in the latest stock market rally. That may be about to change, at least for the short term. Based on options positioning after the company reports results, the stock could head back toward $1,000.

The options market has become so bearish on Eli Lilly shares that it may actually prove bullish. First-quarter results are expected to be strong, with earnings rising by 99.5% to $6.66 per share, on revenue growth of 38.4% to $17.6 billion. Meanwhile, analysts estimate second-quarter earnings growth to slow to 28.1%, to $8.08 per share, with revenue growth down to 26.4%, to $19.66 billion.

Elevated Implied Volatility

Still, put positioning appears to heavily outweigh call positioning, resulting in a negative overall delta and gamma. Normally, options positioning is not an overall problem except when heading into earnings. Currently, implied volatility levels are very high, running above 80%, and are due to fall sharply after the company reports results.

For example, an $865 strike price put expiring on May 1 currently has a premium of about $28 and an IV around 100%. As a result, the stock would need to fall to $837 for the owner of those puts to start earning a profit. But after earnings, the implied volatility for both puts and calls will fall sharply and could even be cut in half. That means the put premium could also decline to roughly $16. So the options immediately start to lose value.

Options Positioning May Favor A Rally

As a result, put premiums may very well start to decay across all strike prices, and market makers may find themselves overhedged against put positioning, potentially forcing them to buy back shares. Holders of puts at lower strike prices may begin unwinding their positions, potentially adding more upward pressure to the stock.

Additionally, a put gamma between the $830 and $850 strike prices may offer support. As the stock declines to support dealer hedging flows, it may require the buying of shares, helping to stabilize the price. However, if it rallies after the result, it could be heading much higher, with the chart showing the largest number of calls across expiration at $1,000.

Also, the at-the-money put option, mentioned earlier, and put options with lower strike prices will have a really tough time seeing the premium rise enough to make them profitable. Unless the company releases horrible results that are truly shocking to the market, pushing the stock prices through all that option-related put support. Therefore, the options positioning appears to favor a move higher, even if it only proves to be short-lived.

Technical Support And Resistance

The technical chart doesn't lay out the support and resistance levels presented in the options position, as one might like. But that is also because the stock had such a violent move higher in October and into November. However, one can see some support from prior highs around $850 and another layer of support from a November gap around $825. Additionally, there are signs of a potential bullish divergence forming with the RSI.

While Lilly has already declined meaningfully over the last several months, the heavy bearish positioning sets up what appears to favor a rebound, potentially toward the $970–$1,000 range—even if it proves to be a post-earnings bounce in the longer term.
